turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kennedy has the opinion of the Court in case 16-373, California Public Employees Retirement System versus ANZ Securities.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: The petitioner, as the Chief Justice has indicated, is California Public Employees Retirement System. It is the largest public pension fund in the United States, and the abbreviation for it often used is CalPERS. In the years 2007 and 2008, CalPERS purchased securities and certain investment offerings underwritten by financial firms, and those firms are the respondents in this case. Later, a suit was brought by investors who had also purchased these securities. They alleged they were material misstatements and omissions in the security's offering documents, this was a class action suit. That suit was timely under the federal statutory time limit that is at issue here, which is a three-year time limit. CalPERS was a member of that putative class. Later, however, CalPERS filed its own suit against the respondents seeking recovery for the same alleged violations. When the class action suit settled, CalPERS withdrew from the class, preferring to rely on its own suit. CalPERS own suit, however, was filed after the three-year period. And the question in this case is whether the CalPERS separate suit was still timely. CalPERS says it was timely because the filing of the class action operated to toll or extend the three years period. The District Court and The Court of Appeals for The Second Circuit disagreed. The separate CalPERS suit was ordered dismissed. This Court now affirms. For reasons explained in the opinion, the Court concludes that the class action suit did not toll the three-year period. The three-year time limit is a statute of repose which demonstrates the legislative intent to give a defendant complete peace from any suit after the time period. The judiciary therefore cannot use its traditional equitable powers to extend the time period. There is a separate argument as well made by CalPERS. It argues that the suit was timely even without regard to the tolling. And this second argument is based on an interpretation of the statute that sets forth the three-year limit. But in this Court's view, the argument is not consistent with the text and meaning of the statute. The opinion goes into considerable detail as to all of these points. For the reasons it sets forth, the judgment of the Court of Appeals is affirmed. Justice Ginsburg has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Breyer, Justice Sotomayor, and Justice Kagan have joined.
